title: Content Providers
objectives: Understand ContentProviders via a real-life analogy
Read data from system providers (Contacts, MediaStore)
Use FileProvider to share files safely
Know when to write your own provider (rare)

sections.markdown:
**Think of a public library's front desk.**

- Behind the desk are all the books (your app's data). Each app has its own private stack — nobody else can walk in.
- If you want another app to READ or WRITE to your stack, you set up a **front desk (ContentProvider)** with a rulebook: 'you can look up books by ID; you can add a new book only if you have a library card (permission); you cannot see the restricted shelf'.
- Every request goes through a URL-like address: `content://com.myapp.provider/books/42`. The desk clerk (ContentResolver) delivers the query to the right provider.
- Android's own system data — **Contacts, Calendar, MediaStore (photos/audio), Downloads, SMS** — is all exposed as ContentProviders. That's why *your* app can 'pick a photo' or 'read a contact' with permission — you're querying the system's front desk.

**In modern apps you rarely WRITE your own ContentProvider anymore** — Room + Retrofit handles internal storage; DI handles cross-module boundaries. But you'll still **READ** from system providers (photos, contacts), and you'll definitely use **`FileProvider`** to share files safely between apps.

**A ContentProvider** exposes structured app data to other processes via `content://` URIs and a uniform CRUD interface (`query`, `insert`, `update`, `delete`). Consumers interact through a **`ContentResolver`**. Each URI has its own permissions. ContentProviders are also required for certain platform integrations (SAF, sync adapters, widgets that read from providers).
## The anatomy

rows: The address: `content://<authority>/<path>/<id>`. Authority is your provider's unique name.
The client side — `context.contentResolver.query(uri, ...)` to talk to any provider.

sections.markdown: ## Reading system providers — the 99% case
## FileProvider — the modern way to share files

sections.code:
<!-- AndroidManifest.xml — declare the FileProvider -->
<provider
  android:name="androidx.core.content.FileProvider"
  android:authorities="${applicationId}.fileprovider"
  android:exported="false"
  android:grantUriPermissions="true">
  <meta-data
    android:name="android.support.FILE_PROVIDER_PATHS"
    android:resource="@xml/file_paths" />
</provider>

<!-- res/xml/file_paths.xml — which internal directories to expose -->
<paths>
  <cache-path name="cache" path="shared/" />
  <files-path name="files" path="exports/" />
</paths>

sections.markdown: ## Writing your own provider — the rare case
## Common beginner mistakes
**Mistake 1: Sharing `file://` URIs.** Since Android 7 (2016), passing a `file://` URI across apps throws `FileUriExposedException`. Use `FileProvider` and `content://` URIs.
**Mistake 2: Building a ContentProvider for internal storage.** Overkill. Use Room + a Repository. Providers are for cross-app or platform integrations.
**Mistake 3: Not closing Cursors.** Leaks a `Cursor` (and its DB connection). Fix: use `?.use { cursor -> ... }` (Kotlin's `Closeable.use`).
**Mistake 4: Missing runtime permission for `READ_CONTACTS` / `READ_MEDIA_IMAGES`.** Provider access to protected data needs BOTH manifest permission AND runtime grant on Android 6+.
**Mistake 5: Forgetting `FLAG_GRANT_READ_URI_PERMISSION`.** Share intents with a content URI must include this flag so the receiver can actually read the file.
## Best practices
**1. Use `FileProvider` for every file-sharing intent.** Never leak `file://` URIs.
**2. Prefer Room + Repository for internal data.** Skip writing a custom ContentProvider unless you're crossing app boundaries.
**3. Always close Cursors with `use`.** Kotlin's stdlib makes this a one-liner.
**4. Prefer new `MediaStore` APIs and the Photo Picker (Android 13+).** Scoped Storage-friendly, no permission required for the Photo Picker.
**5. Use SAF (`ACTION_OPEN_DOCUMENT`) for arbitrary user files.** Avoids the need for `READ_EXTERNAL_STORAGE` permission.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — Read the last 20 contacts** and display their names.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — Share a generated PDF** with an implicit intent using FileProvider.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — Use the Photo Picker (Android 13+)** with `PickVisualMedia` — no permissions needed.
**Practice 4 (Hard) — Query MediaStore for recent photos**, show a grid, tap to open.
**Practice 5 (Expert) — Build a small NotesProvider** backed by Room + custom read/write permissions; consume it from a second app.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is a ContentProvider?**

**A:** A ContentProvider is one of Android's four core components — it exposes an app's data via **`content://` URIs** and a uniform CRUD interface (`query`, `insert`, `update`, `delete`). Consumers use a `ContentResolver` on the client side to talk to any provider. The system's own data (Contacts, Calendar, MediaStore for photos, Downloads, SMS) is exposed as providers — that's how third-party apps can 'pick a photo' or 'list contacts.' In modern app development you rarely WRITE your own provider; you READ from system providers and use `FileProvider` to share files. Custom providers are still relevant for cross-app data, SAF integrations, sync adapters, and certain widget/watch scenarios.

**Q2: How do you query a ContentProvider?**

**A:** Through `ContentResolver`. Example:
```kotlin
contentResolver.query(uri, projection, selection, args, sortOrder)?.use { cursor ->
  while (cursor.moveToNext()) { ... }
}
```
Parameters: **URI** (which provider + path), **projection** (columns to return, like SELECT), **selection + args** (WHERE clause with placeholders), **sortOrder** (ORDER BY). Returns a `Cursor` — you MUST close it (`.use { }`). For protected data (contacts, media), declare the manifest permission AND request it at runtime on Android 6+. The API is nearly identical to raw SQLite, on purpose — it feels familiar.

**Q3: When would you write your own ContentProvider?**

**A:** Real justifications: **(1) Cross-app data sharing** — you want another app to read/write your app's data. **(2) Platform integrations** — Storage Access Framework (SAF) documents, Sync Adapters, some Widgets, custom search suggestions. **(3) Regulatory / firewall reasons** — a well-defined content:// interface with per-URI permissions is easier to audit than an ad-hoc socket. **Not justified:** for internal app storage (use Room + Repository), for observable data streams (use Flow), for private cross-module boundaries (use DI). If a colleague suggests wrapping every table in a ContentProvider 'because Google did in 2013,' push back — modern architecture uses layered repositories, not providers.

**Q4: What is FileProvider and why is it required?**

**A:** `FileProvider` is a subclass of `ContentProvider` (in AndroidX) that serves your app's internal files as `content://` URIs — safely, with temporary permission grants. **Why required:** since Android 7 (Nougat, 2016), sending a `file://` URI across apps throws `FileUriExposedException`. The fix: use `FileProvider.getUriForFile(context, authority, file)`, then attach the resulting `content://` URI to your intent with `FLAG_GRANT_READ_URI_PERMISSION`. The receiving app gets a temporary, permission-scoped view of just that file, not your whole filesystem. Configure allowed paths in `res/xml/file_paths.xml`. This is one of the most commonly-needed patterns in production apps.

**Q5: How does the Photo Picker (Android 13+) change things?**

**A:** Android 13 introduced a system Photo Picker (`ActivityResultContracts.PickVisualMedia()`) that lets users pick photos/videos WITHOUT your app needing `READ_EXTERNAL_STORAGE` or `READ_MEDIA_IMAGES` permission. The system UI runs in a separate process, shows the user's library, returns the picked URI to your app with a temporary read grant. **Why huge:** users often deny broad media permissions but happily pick a single photo. Play now recommends using the Photo Picker over broad permissions. For older devices, Google backports it via Google Play Services. This is a **privacy-first replacement** for the old 'request permission → query MediaStore' flow, and it's the modern default for photo-picking use cases.

## Real-world usage

- **Photo pickers** — every image-sharing app uses the Photo Picker or MediaStore.
- **Contact pickers** — dialers, chat apps, invitation flows.
- **File attachment / share intents** — email, cloud storage, note-taking apps.
- **FileProvider ubiquity** — anywhere your app hands a file to another app.
- **Storage Access Framework (SAF)** — 'open a document from anywhere' using `ACTION_OPEN_DOCUMENT`.
- **Sync Adapters** — legacy pattern for periodic sync; used inside contacts/calendar/photos historically.
- **Widgets that show data** from an app's provider (some launchers).

## Advanced notes

- **`ContentObserver`** — listen for changes to a URI; used internally by RoomLiveData / Flow adapters.
- **UriMatcher** — helper for parsing incoming URIs (`/notes` vs `/notes/*` etc).
- **`ContentProviderClient`** — reuse a client for many calls without re-resolving the authority.
- **Batch operations** — `applyBatch(operations)` to run multiple CRUD ops atomically.
- **Sync Adapters** — deprecated for most use cases; WorkManager is the modern replacement.
- **`DocumentsProvider`** — a special ContentProvider for SAF file-picker integration.
- **Cross-process permissions** — declare custom `<permission>` in your manifest; grant selectively per URI with `grantUriPermission`.
- **Cursor limitations** — `Bundle`-based results (Room) beat Cursor in modern reactive apps; providers exposing Cursor still needed for legacy consumers.

flashcards.back: Exposes app data across process boundaries via content:// URIs + CRUD.
Cross-app sharing / SAF / Sync Adapters. Rarely for internal storage — use Room.
PickVisualMedia — no media permission required; privacy-first UX.

quiz.q: Since Android 7, sharing file:// URIs throws…

challenges.text: Query the Contacts provider and list display names.
Query MediaStore for recent photos and show them in a grid.
Use FileProvider to share a generated PDF.
Use the Android 13 Photo Picker (PickVisualMedia contract).
Build a NotesProvider backed by Room; consume from a second app.
Register a ContentObserver on MediaStore and react to new photos.
Implement a DocumentsProvider for SAF file-picker integration.
